title: Satellite navigation

infobox.value: | | --- | | [Robotic spacecraft](/wiki/Robotic_spacecraft "Robotic spacecraft")  * [Satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") * [Space probe](/wiki/Space_probe "Space probe") * [Cargo spacecraft](/wiki/Cargo_spacecraft "Cargo spacecraft") |  |  | | --- | | [Crewed spacecraft](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight")  * [Apollo Lunar Module](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Module "Apollo Lunar Module") * [Space capsules](/wiki/Space_capsule "Space capsule") * [Space Shuttle](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle") * [Space stations](/wiki/Space_station "Space station") * [Spaceplanes](/wiki/Spaceplane "Spaceplane") * [Vostok](/wiki/Vostok_programme "Vostok programme") |

(**satnav**) or **satellite positioning** is the use of [satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") for [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") or [geopositioning](/wiki/Geopositioning "Geopositioning"). A **global navigation satellite system** (**GNSS**) provides coverage for any user on Earth, including air, land, and sea. There are four operational GNSS systems: the United States [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS), Russia's Global Navigation Satellite System ([GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS")), China's [BeiDou Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") (BDS)[[1]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) and the European Union's [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Furthermore, there are two **regional navigation satellite systems** (**RNSS**) in the form of Japan's Quasi-Zenith Satellite System ([QZSS](/wiki/QZSS "QZSS")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System ([IRNSS](/wiki/IRNSS "IRNSS"), also known as NavIC).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
(PNT). Satnav systems operate independently of any telephonic or internet reception, though these technologies can enhance the usefulness of the positioning information generated.

is the first generation system and is the combination of existing satellite navigation systems (GPS and GLONASS), with [satellite-based augmentation systems](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) or [ground-based augmentation systems](/wiki/Ground-based_augmentation_system "Ground-based augmentation system") (GBAS).[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7) In the United States, the satellite-based component is the [Wide Area Augmentation System](/wiki/Wide_Area_Augmentation_System "Wide Area Augmentation System") (WAAS); in Europe, it is the [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service") (EGNOS); in Japan, it is the [Multi-Functional Satellite Augmentation System](/wiki/MTSAT_Satellite_Augmentation_System "MTSAT Satellite Augmentation System") (MSAS); and in India, it is the [GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation](/wiki/GPS-aided_GEO_augmented_navigation "GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation") (GAGAN). Ground-based augmentation is provided by systems like the [local-area augmentation system](/wiki/Local-area_augmentation_system "Local-area augmentation system") (LAAS).[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7)

is the second generation of systems that independently provide a full civilian satellite navigation system, exemplified by the European Galileo positioning system.[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7) These systems will provide the accuracy and integrity monitoring necessary for civil navigation; including aircraft. Initially, this system consisted of only Upper [L Band](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") frequency sets (L1 for GPS, E1 for Galileo, [and](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") G1 for GLONASS). In recent years, GNSS systems have begun activating Lower [L Band](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") frequency sets (L2 and L5 for GPS, E5a and E5b for Galileo, [and](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") G3 for GLONASS) for civilian use; they feature higher aggregate accuracy and fewer problems with signal reflection.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-9) As of late 2018, a few consumer-grade GNSS devices are being sold that use both. They are typically called "Dual-band GNSS" or "Dual-band GPS" devices.

The [US Space Force](/wiki/US_Space_Force "US Space Force")'s [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") was the first global satellite navigation system and the first to be provided as a free global service.

**Satellite navigation** (**satnav**) or **satellite positioning** is the use of [satellites](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite") for [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") or [geopositioning](/wiki/Geopositioning "Geopositioning"). A **global navigation satellite system** (**GNSS**) provides coverage for any user on Earth, including air, land, and sea. There are four operational GNSS systems: the United States [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS), Russia's Global Navigation Satellite System ([GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS")), China's [BeiDou Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") (BDS)[[1]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-1) and the European Union's [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Furthermore, there are two **regional navigation satellite systems** (**RNSS**) in the form of Japan's Quasi-Zenith Satellite System ([QZSS](/wiki/QZSS "QZSS")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System ([IRNSS](/wiki/IRNSS "IRNSS"), also known as NavIC).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A *[satellite-based augmentation system](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system")* (SBAS) is a system that is designed to enhance the accuracy of the global GNSS systems.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) The SBAS systems include Japan's [Quasi-Zenith Satellite System](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System") (QZSS),[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6) India's [GAGAN](/wiki/GAGAN "GAGAN"), and the European [EGNOS](/wiki/EGNOS "EGNOS"), all of them based on GPS.

[Satellite navigation devices](/wiki/Satellite_navigation_device "Satellite navigation device") determine their location ([longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), and [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")/[elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation")) to high precision (within a few centimeters to meters) using [time signals](/wiki/Time_signal "Time signal") transmitted along a [line of sight](/wiki/Line-of-sight_propagation "Line-of-sight propagation") by [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio") from satellites. The system can be used for providing position, navigation or for tracking the position of something fitted with a receiver (satellite tracking). The signals also allow the electronic receiver to calculate the current local time to a high precision, which allows time synchronisation. These uses are collectively known as **Positioning, Navigation and Timing** (PNT). Satnav systems operate independently of any telephonic or internet reception, though these technologies can enhance the usefulness of the positioning information generated.

Global coverage for each system is generally achieved by a [satellite constellation](/wiki/Satellite_constellation "Satellite constellation") of 18–30 [medium Earth orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") (MEO) satellites spread between several [orbital planes](/wiki/Orbital_planes "Orbital planes"). The actual systems vary, but all use [orbital inclinations](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination") of >50° and [orbital periods](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period") of roughly twelve hours (at an altitude of about 20,000 kilometres or 12,000 miles).[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Further information: [GNSS augmentation](/wiki/GNSS_augmentation "GNSS augmentation")

GNSS systems that provide enhanced accuracy and integrity monitoring usable for civil navigation are classified as follows:[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7)

* **GNSS-1** is the first generation system and is the combination of existing satellite navigation systems (GPS and GLONASS), with [satellite-based augmentation systems](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) or [ground-based augmentation systems](/wiki/Ground-based_augmentation_system "Ground-based augmentation system") (GBAS).[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7) In the United States, the satellite-based component is the [Wide Area Augmentation System](/wiki/Wide_Area_Augmentation_System "Wide Area Augmentation System") (WAAS); in Europe, it is the [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service") (EGNOS); in Japan, it is the [Multi-Functional Satellite Augmentation System](/wiki/MTSAT_Satellite_Augmentation_System "MTSAT Satellite Augmentation System") (MSAS); and in India, it is the [GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation](/wiki/GPS-aided_GEO_augmented_navigation "GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation") (GAGAN). Ground-based augmentation is provided by systems like the [local-area augmentation system](/wiki/Local-area_augmentation_system "Local-area augmentation system") (LAAS).[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7)
* **GNSS-2** is the second generation of systems that independently provide a full civilian satellite navigation system, exemplified by the European Galileo positioning system.[[7]](#cite_note-generations-7) These systems will provide the accuracy and integrity monitoring necessary for civil navigation; including aircraft. Initially, this system consisted of only Upper [L Band](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") frequency sets (L1 for GPS, E1 for Galileo, [and](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") G1 for GLONASS). In recent years, GNSS systems have begun activating Lower [L Band](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") frequency sets (L2 and L5 for GPS, E5a and E5b for Galileo, [and](/wiki/L_Band "L Band") G3 for GLONASS) for civilian use; they feature higher aggregate accuracy and fewer problems with signal reflection.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-9) As of late 2018, a few consumer-grade GNSS devices are being sold that use both. They are typically called "Dual-band GNSS" or "Dual-band GPS" devices.

By their roles in the navigation system, systems can be classified as:

* There are four global satellite navigation systems, currently [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (United States), [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS") (Russian Federation), [BeiDou](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") (China) and [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") (European Union).
* Satellite-based augmentation systems (SBAS) such as [OmniSTAR](/wiki/OmniSTAR "OmniSTAR") and [StarFire](/wiki/StarFire_(navigation_system) "StarFire (navigation system)").
* Regional SBAS, including WAAS (US), EGNOS (EU), MSAS (Japan), [GAGAN](/wiki/GPS-aided_GEO_augmented_navigation "GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation") (India) and SDCM (Russia).
* Regional navigation satellite systems (RNSS) such as India's [NAVIC](/wiki/Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System "Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System") and Japan's [QZSS](/wiki/QZSS "QZSS").
* Continental-scale Ground-Based Augmentation Systems (GBAS); for example, the Australian GRAS and the joint US Coast Guard, Canadian Coast Guard, US Army Corps of Engineers and US Department of Transportation National [Differential GPS](/wiki/Differential_GPS "Differential GPS") (DGPS) service.
* Regional-scale GBAS such as CORS networks.
* Local GBAS typified by a single GPS reference station operating [Real Time Kinematic](/wiki/Real_Time_Kinematic "Real Time Kinematic") (RTK) corrections.

As many of the global GNSS systems (and augmentation systems) use similar frequencies and signals around L1, many "Multi-GNSS" receivers capable of using multiple systems have been produced. While some systems strive to interoperate with GPS as well as possible by providing the same clock, others do not.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [GPS § History](/wiki/GPS#History "GPS"), [GLONASS § History](/wiki/GLONASS#History "GLONASS"), [GALILEO#History](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation)#History "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), and [BeiDou § History](/wiki/BeiDou#History "BeiDou")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Accuracy_of_Navigation_Systems.svg/250px-Accuracy_of_Navigation_Systems.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Accuracy_of_Navigation_Systems.svg)

Ground-based [radio navigation](/wiki/Radio_navigation "Radio navigation") is decades old. The [DECCA](/wiki/Decca_Navigator_System "Decca Navigator System"), [LORAN](/wiki/LORAN "LORAN"), [GEE](/wiki/Gee_(navigation) "Gee (navigation)") and [Omega](/wiki/Omega_Navigation_System "Omega Navigation System") systems used terrestrial [longwave](/wiki/Longwave "Longwave") radio [transmitters](/wiki/Transmitter "Transmitter") which broadcast a radio pulse from a known "master" location, followed by a pulse repeated from a number of "slave" stations. The delay between the reception of the master signal and the slave signals allowed the receiver to deduce the distance to each of the slaves, providing a [fix](/wiki/Fix_(position) "Fix (position)").

The first satellite navigation system was [Transit](/wiki/Transit_(satellite) "Transit (satellite)"), a system deployed by the US military in the 1960s. Transit's operation was based on the [Doppler effect](/wiki/Doppler_effect "Doppler effect"): the satellites travelled on well-known paths and broadcast their signals on a well-known [radio frequency](/wiki/Radio_frequency "Radio frequency"). The received frequency will differ slightly from the broadcast frequency because of the movement of the satellite with respect to the receiver. By monitoring this frequency shift over a short time interval, the receiver can determine its location to one side or the other of the satellite, and several such measurements combined with a precise knowledge of the satellite's orbit can fix a particular position. Satellite orbital position errors are caused by radio-wave [refraction](/wiki/Refraction "Refraction"), gravity field changes (as the Earth's gravitational field is not uniform), and other phenomena. A team, led by Harold L Jury of Pan Am Aerospace Division in Florida from 1970 to 1973, found solutions or corrections for many error sources.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Using real-time data and recursive estimation, the systematic and residual errors were narrowed down to accuracy sufficient for navigation.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Principles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Principles")]

Further information: [GPS § Principles](/wiki/GPS#Principles "GPS"), and [GPS § Navigation equations](/wiki/GPS#Navigation_equations "GPS")

Part of an orbiting satellite's broadcast includes its precise orbital data. Originally, the [US Naval Observatory (USNO)](/wiki/US_Naval_Observatory "US Naval Observatory") continuously observed the precise orbits of these satellites. As a satellite's orbit deviated, the USNO sent the updated information to the satellite. Subsequent broadcasts from an updated satellite would contain its most recent [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris").

Modern systems are more direct. The satellite broadcasts a signal that contains orbital data (from which the position of the satellite can be calculated) and the precise time the signal was transmitted. Orbital data include a rough [almanac](/wiki/Almanac "Almanac") for all satellites to aid in finding them, and a precise ephemeris for this satellite. The orbital [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") is transmitted in a data message that is superimposed on a code that serves as a timing reference. The satellite uses an [atomic clock](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") to maintain synchronization of all the satellites in the constellation. The receiver compares the time of broadcast encoded in the transmission of three (at sea level) or four (which allows an altitude calculation also) different satellites, measuring the time-of-flight to each satellite. Several such measurements can be made at the same time to different satellites, allowing a continual fix to be generated in real time using an adapted version of [trilateration](/wiki/Trilateration "Trilateration"): see [GNSS positioning calculation](/wiki/GNSS_positioning_calculation "GNSS positioning calculation") for details.

Each distance measurement, regardless of the system being used, places the receiver on a spherical shell centred on the broadcaster, at the measured distance from the broadcaster. By taking several such measurements and then looking for a point where the shells meet, a fix is generated. However, in the case of fast-moving receivers, the position of the receiver moves as signals are received from several satellites. In addition, the radio signals slow slightly as they pass through the ionosphere, and this slowing varies with the receiver's angle to the satellite, because that angle corresponds to the distance which the signal travels through the ionosphere. The basic computation thus attempts to find the shortest directed line tangent to four oblate spherical shells centred on four satellites. Satellite navigation receivers reduce errors by using combinations of signals from multiple satellites and multiple correlators, and then using techniques such as [Kalman filtering](/wiki/Kalman_filter "Kalman filter") to combine the noisy, partial, and constantly changing data into a single estimate for position, time, and velocity.

[Einstein](/wiki/Einstein "Einstein")'s theory of [general relativity](/wiki/General_relativity "General relativity") is applied to GPS time correction, the net result is that time on a GPS satellite clock advances faster than a clock on the ground by about 38 microseconds per day.[[12]](#cite_note-einstein-12)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp/250px-GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:GPSTest_screenshot_(2025).webp)

GNSS satellites (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and BeiDou) and a GNSS augmentation system (Quasi-Zenith Satellite System) used on a smartphone in [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") (2025)

Main article: [GNSS applications](/wiki/GNSS_applications "GNSS applications")

Further information: [Automotive navigation system](/wiki/Automotive_navigation_system "Automotive navigation system")

The original motivation for satellite navigation was for military applications. Satellite navigation allows precision in the delivery of weapons to targets, greatly increasing their lethality whilst reducing inadvertent casualties from mis-directed weapons. (See [Guided bomb](/wiki/Guided_bomb "Guided bomb")). Satellite navigation also allows forces to be directed and to locate themselves more easily, reducing the [fog of war](/wiki/Fog_of_war "Fog of war").

Now a global navigation satellite system, such as [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), is used to determine users location and the location of other people or objects at any given moment. The range of application of satellite navigation in the future is enormous, including both the public and private sectors across numerous market segments such as science, transport, agriculture, etc.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The ability to supply satellite navigation signals is also the ability to deny their availability. The operator of a satellite navigation system potentially has the ability to degrade or eliminate satellite navigation services over any territory it desires.

Launched GNSS satellites 1978 to 2014

In order of first launch year:

### GPS (1978)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: GPS (1978)")]

Main article: [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System")

The United States' Global Positioning System (GPS) consists of up to 32 [medium Earth orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") satellites in six different [orbital planes](/wiki/Orbital_plane_(astronomy) "Orbital plane (astronomy)"). The exact number of satellites varies as older satellites are retired and replaced. Operational since 1978 and globally available since 1994, GPS is the world's most used satellite navigation system.

### GLONASS (1982)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: GLONASS (1982)")]

Main article: [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS")

The formerly [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), and now [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), *Global'naya Navigatsionnaya Sputnikovaya Sistema*, (Global Navigation Satellite System or GLONASS), is a space-based satellite navigation system that provides a civilian radionavigation-satellite service and is also used by the Russian Aerospace Defence Forces. GLONASS has full global coverage since 1995 and with 24 active satellites.

### BeiDou (2000)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: BeiDou (2000)")]

Main article: [BeiDou Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/BeiDou_Navigation_Satellite_System "BeiDou Navigation Satellite System")

BeiDou started as the now-decommissioned Beidou-1, an Asia-Pacific local network on the geostationary orbits. The second generation of the system BeiDou-2 became operational in China in December 2011.[[14]](#cite_note-16) The BeiDou-3 system is proposed to consist of 30 [MEO](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") satellites and five geostationary satellites (IGSO). A 16-satellite regional version (covering Asia and Pacific area) was completed by December 2012. Global service was completed by December 2018.[[15]](#cite_note-17) On 23 June 2020, the BDS-3 constellation deployment is fully completed after the last satellite was successfully launched at the [Xichang Satellite Launch Center](/wiki/Xichang_Satellite_Launch_Center "Xichang Satellite Launch Center").[[16]](#cite_note-18)

### Galileo (2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Galileo (2011)")]

Main article: [Galileo (satellite navigation)](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)")

The [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") agreed in March 2002 to introduce their own alternative to GPS, called the [Galileo positioning system](/wiki/Galileo_positioning_system "Galileo positioning system"). Galileo became operational on 15 December 2016 (global Early Operational Capability, EOC).[[17]](#cite_note-19) At an estimated cost of €10 billion,[[18]](#cite_note-20) the system of 30 [MEO](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") satellites was originally scheduled to be operational in 2010. The original year to become operational was 2014.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The first experimental satellite was launched on 28 December 2005.[[20]](#cite_note-22) Galileo is expected to be compatible with the [modernized GPS](/wiki/GPS_modernization "GPS modernization") system. The receivers will be able to combine the signals from both Galileo and GPS satellites to greatly increase the accuracy. The full Galileo constellation consists of 24 active satellites,[[21]](#cite_note-23) the last of which was launched in December 2021.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-25) The main modulation used in Galileo Open Service signal is the [Composite Binary Offset Carrier](/wiki/Composite_Binary_Offset_Carrier "Composite Binary Offset Carrier") (CBOC) modulation.

## Regional navigation satellite systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Regional navigation satellite systems")]

### NavIC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: NavIC")]

Main article: [NavIC](/wiki/NavIC "NavIC")

The **NavIC** (Navigation with Indian Constellation) is an autonomous regional satellite navigation system developed by the [Indian Space Research Organisation](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation") (ISRO). The [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") approved the project in May 2006. It consists of a constellation of seven navigational satellites.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Three of the satellites are placed in [geostationary orbit (GEO)](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit") and the remaining four in [geosynchronous orbit (GSO)](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") to have a larger signal footprint and lower number of satellites to map the region. It is intended to provide an all-weather absolute position accuracy of better than 7.6 metres (25 ft) throughout [India](/wiki/India "India") and within a region extending approximately 1,500 km (900 mi) around it.[[25]](#cite_note-27) An Extended Service Area lies between the primary service area and a rectangle area enclosed by the [30th parallel south](/wiki/30th_parallel_south "30th parallel south") to the [50th parallel north](/wiki/50th_parallel_north "50th parallel north") and the [30th meridian east](/wiki/30th_meridian_east "30th meridian east") to the [130th meridian east](/wiki/130th_meridian_east "130th meridian east"), 1,500 to 6,000 km (900 to 3,700 mi) beyond borders.[[26]](#cite_note-28) A goal of complete Indian control has been stated, with the [space segment](/wiki/Satellite_space_segment "Satellite space segment"), [ground segment](/wiki/Ground_segment "Ground segment") and user receivers all being built in India.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

The constellation was in orbit as of 2018, and the system was available for public use in early 2018.[[28]](#cite_note-30) NavIC provides two levels of service, the "standard positioning service", which will be open for civilian use, and a "restricted service" (an [encrypted](/wiki/Encryption "Encryption") one) for authorized users (including military). There are plans to expand NavIC system by increasing constellation size from 7 to 11.[[29]](#cite_note-:0-31)

India plans to make the NavIC global by adding 24 more [MEO](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") satellites. The Global NavIC will be free to use for the global public.[[30]](#cite_note-32)

The first two generations of China's BeiDou navigation system were designed to provide regional coverage.

## Augmentation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Augmentation")]

[GNSS augmentation](/wiki/GNSS_augmentation "GNSS augmentation") is a method of improving a navigation system's attributes, such as accuracy, reliability, and availability, through the integration of external information into the calculation process, for example, the [Wide Area Augmentation System](/wiki/Wide_Area_Augmentation_System "Wide Area Augmentation System"), the [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service"), the [Multi-functional Satellite Augmentation System](/wiki/Multi-functional_Satellite_Augmentation_System "Multi-functional Satellite Augmentation System"), [Differential GPS](/wiki/Differential_GPS "Differential GPS"), [GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation](/wiki/GPS-aided_GEO_augmented_navigation "GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation") (GAGAN) and [inertial navigation systems](/wiki/Inertial_navigation_system "Inertial navigation system").

### QZSS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: QZSS")]

Main article: [Quasi-Zenith Satellite System](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System")

The Quasi-Zenith Satellite System (QZSS) is a four-satellite regional [time transfer](/wiki/Time_transfer "Time transfer") system and enhancement for [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") covering [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and the [Asia-Oceania](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") regions. QZSS services were available on a trial basis as of January 12, 2018, and were started in November 2018. The first satellite was launched in September 2010.[[31]](#cite_note-:2-33) An independent satellite navigation system (from GPS) with 7 satellites is planned for 2023.[[32]](#cite_note-34)

### EGNOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: EGNOS")]

This section is an excerpt from [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/EGNOS_map.svg/250px-EGNOS_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EGNOS_map.svg)

Map of the EGNOS ground network

The [European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service](/wiki/European_Geostationary_Navigation_Overlay_Service "European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service") (EGNOS) is a [satellite-based augmentation system](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (SBAS) developed by the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") and [Eurocontrol](/wiki/Eurocontrol "Eurocontrol") on behalf of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"). Currently, it supplements [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") by reporting on the reliability and accuracy of their positioning data and sending out corrections. The system will supplement [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") in the future version 3.0.

EGNOS consists of 40 Ranging Integrity Monitoring Stations, 2 Mission Control Centres, 6 Navigation Land Earth Stations, the EGNOS Wide Area Network (EWAN), and 3 [geostationary satellites](/wiki/Geostationary_satellite "Geostationary satellite").[[33]](#cite_note-35) Ground stations determine the accuracy of the satellite navigation systems data and transfer it to the geostationary satellites; users may freely obtain this data from those satellites using an EGNOS-enabled receiver, or over the Internet. One main use of the system is in [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation").

According to specifications, horizontal position accuracy when using EGNOS-provided corrections should be better than seven metres. In practice, the horizontal position accuracy is at the metre level.

Similar service is provided in North America by the [Wide Area Augmentation System](/wiki/Wide_Area_Augmentation_System "Wide Area Augmentation System") (WAAS), in Russia by the [System for Differential Corrections and Monitoring](/wiki/System_for_Differential_Corrections_and_Monitoring "System for Differential Corrections and Monitoring") (SDCM), and in Asia, by Japan's [Multi-functional Satellite Augmentation System](/wiki/Multi-functional_Satellite_Augmentation_System "Multi-functional Satellite Augmentation System") (MSAS) and India's [GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation](/wiki/GPS-aided_GEO_augmented_navigation "GPS-aided GEO augmented navigation") (GAGAN).

[Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") and EGNOS received a budget of €14.6 billion for its six-year, 2021–2027, research and development period.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

Using multiple GNSS systems for user positioning increases the number of visible satellites, improves precise point positioning (PPP) and shortens the average convergence time.[[43]](#cite_note-45)
The signal-in-space ranging error (SISRE) in November 2019 were 1.6 cm for Galileo, 2.3 cm for GPS, 5.2 cm for GLONASS and 5.5 cm for BeiDou when using real-time corrections for satellite orbits and clocks.[[44]](#cite_note-46) The average SISREs of the BDS-3 MEO, IGSO, and GEO satellites were 0.52 m, 0.90 m and 1.15 m, respectively. Compared to the four major global satellite navigation systems consisting of MEO satellites, the SISRE of the BDS-3 MEO satellites was slightly inferior to 0.4 m of Galileo, slightly superior to 0.59 m of GPS, and remarkably superior to 2.33 m of GLONASS. The SISRE of BDS-3 IGSO was 0.90 m, which was on par with the 0.92 m of QZSS IGSO. However, as the BDS-3 GEO satellites were newly launched and not completely functioning in orbit, their average SISRE was marginally worse than the 0.91 m of the QZSS GEO satellites.[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)

## Related techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Related techniques")]

Further information: [Satellite geodesy § Radio techniques](/wiki/Satellite_geodesy#Radio_techniques "Satellite geodesy")

### DORIS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: DORIS")]

Main article: [DORIS (satellite system)](/wiki/DORIS_(satellite_system) "DORIS (satellite system)")

Doppler Orbitography and Radio-Positioning Integrated by Satellite (DORIS) is a French precision navigation system. Unlike other GNSS systems, it is based on static emitting stations around the world, the receivers being on satellites, in order to precisely determine their orbital position. The system may be used also for mobile receivers on land with more limited usage and coverage. Used with traditional GNSS systems, it pushes the accuracy of positions to centimetric precision (and to millimetric precision for altimetric application and also allows monitoring very tiny seasonal changes of Earth rotation and deformations), in order to build a much more precise geodesic reference system.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### LEO satellites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: LEO satellites")]

The two current operational [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") (LEO) [satellite phone](/wiki/Satellite_phone "Satellite phone") networks are able to track transceiver units with accuracy of a few kilometres using doppler shift calculations from the satellite. The coordinates are sent back to the transceiver unit where they can be read using [AT commands](/wiki/AT_command "AT command") or a [graphical user interface](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface").[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) This can also be used by the gateway to enforce restrictions on geographically bound calling plans.

## International regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: International regulation")]

The [International Telecommunication Union](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") (ITU) defines a **radionavigation-satellite service** (**RNSS**) as "a [radiodetermination-satellite service](/wiki/Radiodetermination-satellite_service "Radiodetermination-satellite service") used for the purpose of [radionavigation](/wiki/Radio_navigation "Radio navigation"). This service may also include [feeder links](/wiki/Feeder_link "Feeder link") necessary for its operation".[[48]](#cite_note-50)

RNSS is regarded as a [safety-of-life service](/wiki/Safety_service "Safety service") and an essential part of [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") which must be protected from [interferences](/wiki/Interference_(communication) "Interference (communication)").

**Aeronautical radionavigation-satellite**  (**ARNSS**) is – according to *Article 1.47* of the [International Telecommunication Union's](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") (ITU) [Radio Regulations](/wiki/ITU_Radio_Regulations "ITU Radio Regulations") (RR)[[49]](#cite_note-51) – defined as «*A [radionavigation service](/wiki/Radionavigation_service "Radionavigation service") in which [earth stations](/wiki/Earth_station "Earth station") are located on board aircraft*.»

**Maritime radionavigation-satellite service**  (**MRNSS**) is – according to *Article 1.45* of the [International Telecommunication Union's](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") (ITU) [Radio Regulations](/wiki/ITU_Radio_Regulations "ITU Radio Regulations") (RR)[[50]](#cite_note-52) – defined as «*A [radionavigation-satellite service](/wiki/Radionavigation-satellite_service "Radionavigation-satellite service") in which earth stations are located on board ships*.»

### Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Classification")]

ITU Radio Regulations (article 1) classifies [radiocommunication](/wiki/Radio "Radio") services as:

* [Radiodetermination service](/wiki/Radiodetermination_service "Radiodetermination service") (article 1.40)
* [Radiodetermination-satellite service](/wiki/Radiodetermination-satellite_service "Radiodetermination-satellite service") (article 1.41)
* [Radionavigation service](/wiki/Radionavigation_service "Radionavigation service") (article 1.42)
  + **Radionavigation-satellite service** (article 1.43)
  + [Maritime radionavigation service](/wiki/Maritime_radionavigation_service "Maritime radionavigation service") (article 1.44)
    - [Maritime radionavigation-satellite service](/wiki/Maritime_radionavigation-satellite_service "Maritime radionavigation-satellite service") (article 1.45)
  + [Aeronautical radionavigation service](/wiki/Aeronautical_radionavigation_service "Aeronautical radionavigation service") (article 1.46)
    - [Aeronautical radionavigation-satellite service](/wiki/Aeronautical_radionavigation-satellite_service "Aeronautical radionavigation-satellite service") (article 1.47)

Examples of RNSS use

* Augmentation system [GNSS augmentation](/wiki/GNSS_augmentation "GNSS augmentation")
* [Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast](/wiki/Automatic_Dependent_Surveillance%E2%80%93Broadcast "Automatic Dependent Surveillance–Broadcast")
* [BeiDou Navigation Satellite System](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") (BDS)
* [GALILEO](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), European GNSS
* [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS), with [Differential GPS](/wiki/Differential_GPS "Differential GPS") (DGPS)
* [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS")
* [NAVIC](/wiki/Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System "Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System")
* [Quasi-Zenith Satellite System](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System") (QZSS)

### Frequency allocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Satellite_navigation&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Frequency allocation")]

Further information: [Frequency allocation](/wiki/Frequency_allocation "Frequency allocation")

The allocation of radio frequencies is provided according to *Article 5* of the ITU Radio Regulations (edition 2012).[[51]](#cite_note-53)

To improve harmonisation in spectrum usage, most service allocations are incorporated in national Tables of Frequency Allocations and Utilisations within the responsibility of the appropriate national administration. Allocations are:

* primary: indicated by writing in capital letters
* secondary: indicated by small letters
* exclusive or shared usage: within the responsibility of administrations.
